finding: `score_code_novelty` is specified to bypass `score_novelty` (which includes token-budget chunking) to gain access to `matched_prior_id`, but the plan only says it 'mirrors' `_score_floor_novelty` wiring without explicitly specifying whether it re-implements the chunking from `verify_request_chunks`. If chunking is omitted, `score_code_novelty` will silently send the entire advisory set in one over-budget sub-call when advisory findings are numerous, causing the novelty sub-call to fail or produce garbage — and the fail-safe (empty map → novelty 0.0 → carryover → no drops) would then suppress all drops rather than correctly scoring them.
suggested_fix: Add to the plan: 'score_code_novelty wraps the full chunked sub-call loop (mirroring score_novelty's verify_request_chunks + per-chunk run loop), but collects both the novelty float AND matched_prior_id from reshape_novelties in the same loop — the only divergence from score_novelty is the richer return type {index: (float, str)}; the chunking, fail-safe, and window budget are identical.'
location: What section: 'REUSE unchanged kernel primitives' / 'NOTE: it does NOT reuse the kernel score_novelty wrapper verbatim'
rationale: The plan explicitly states score_code_novelty 'mirrors' the runner wiring and reuses primitives, but never specifies whether it replicates the chunking/verify_request_chunks loop; if omitted, over-budget sub-calls could silently trigger the empty-map fail-safe and suppress all drops — a real, actionable gap worth surfacing.

finding: The Alternatives section rejects 'Tag- or progress-note-based suppression' explicitly because it is 'unsigned + gameable (prompt-injection surface into a security-sensitive gate)', yet AC4's second delivered-now path — 'superseded by a sibling under the same parent' (in-epic sibling supersede) — carries no described signing, verification, or tamper-resistance mechanism. This is a cross-section contradiction: the security rationale used to reject unsigned suppression paths is not applied to the sibling-supersede path that is accepted.
suggested_fix: Add to AC4: 'sibling supersede is valid only when the sibling's own completion attestation covers the AC text of the superseded child (verified by attestation hash comparison), not by any unsigned metadata field' — or similar language that closes the gap between the security rationale in Alternatives and the AC4 definition.
location: Alternatives considered (tag-suppression rejection) vs AC4 (delivered-now definition)
rationale: The plan rejects unsigned suppression on security grounds but doesn't specify a verification/signing mechanism for the sibling-supersede delivered-now path; whether this is a genuine coherence gap or a non-issue (structural graph relationship vs. gameable text field) is underdetermined by the plan text.

finding: No concrete validation mechanism exists in any AC: none of the three ACs specify a before/after workflow comparison, an operational metric, or a dogfooding step showing the check actually catches real defects that previously slipped through.
suggested_fix: Add to AC1: 'Validated against a fixture set of ≥N known-bad verify lines (pure inspection, unanchored grep, unquoted metachar) where all N must be flagged, and ≥M known-good lines where all M must pass or abstain.'
location: Acceptance Criteria (all three ACs)
rationale: AC2 already specifies a fixture set covering all three defect classes plus an unparseable line with a unit test asserting fires/abstains, which is precisely the concrete validation mechanism the finding claims is absent.

finding: The 'blast_radius only LOWERS, never raises' ratchet rule is described in the plan as a processing decision rule to add to both verifier prompts and the SeverityAttrs.blast_radius doc in verify.py, but the plan never specifies the exact phrasing. The SeverityAttrs schema (verify.py:116) defines the enum vocabulary but carries no co-located ratchet rule; the plan's AC[2] grep proof ('never raises') depends on a literal string the plan itself never quotes.
suggested_fix: Add to the SeverityAttrs.blast_radius Field description: 'local|module|system — RATCHET: blast_radius can only LOWER severity, never raise it; a grand subject does not widen a local flaw.' Mirror the same sentence in both verifier prompt blast_radius bullets.
location: What section / AC[2]: plan_review_verifier.md, plan_review_verifier_agentic.md, SeverityAttrs.blast_radius in src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify.py:116
rationale: The plan does specify the literal grep token ('never raises') in AC[2] and instructs making that phrase explicit and uniform across all three locations; the exact full sentence wording is a minor stylistic gap, not a genuine testability/coherence defect under T8.

finding: The Design section describes the pending computation as 'cached boolean' yet the hint is required to name permanently-failing units ('a permanently-failing unit stays out of the set and is named in the rate-limited hint'). A boolean cannot carry the id list needed to compose that message, creating an internal contradiction an executor must silently resolve.
suggested_fix: Replace 'computed ONCE per process (cached boolean)' with 'computed ONCE per process (cached frozenset of pending ids; truthiness used as the gate, ids used in the hint message)'.
location: Design section — 'pending = registry ids − parsed .ensure-applied, computed ONCE per process (cached boolean)' and 'Accepted trade-offs' paragraph
rationale: The plan stores pending as a 'cached boolean' yet the trade-offs section promises the hint names the specific permanently-failing unit, which requires the id list; a boolean cannot carry those ids, a genuine internal contradiction the executor must resolve.

finding: The plan does not define what 'thin assertion' means for already-covered leaves, leaving the minimum acceptable matrix entry for those leaves undefined; an agent cannot know whether a single `assert result is not None` satisfies 'thin' or whether it requires matching the same effect dimensions as fully-covered leaves.
suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the 'What' section: 'A thin entry must, at minimum, assert the expected `ApplyResult` status and verify the primary mock call was invoked; it may omit error-path and follow-on assertions when those are fully covered by the referenced existing test.'
location: What section — 'the matrix entry may be a thin assertion that references the same observable effect'
rationale: The 'thin assertion' allowance in the What section is genuinely underspecified and appears to conflict with the strict per-entry assertion requirements in the ACs, leaving an agent unable to determine the minimum acceptable entry for already-covered leaves.

finding: list_epics lib-shim removal is claimed by two children simultaneously: child 7c92 lists 'lib rebar.list_epics() -> list_tickets(ticket_type=\'epic\')' as one of its 7 scheduled registry shims to remove, and child 5899 also owns removal of the entire list_epics family (lib + MCP + CLI + schema). This is a scope overlap where the same deletion has two owners.
suggested_fix: Remove `lib rebar.list_epics()` from child 7c92's shim list (noting it is handled by 5899 which covers the full family). Update 7c92's NOTE to reference 5899 for this item.
location: child 7c92-25af-0dbd-44f2 (AC: 'The 7 scheduled shims ... are removed') and child 5899-4318-84cc-43cb (AC: 'list_epics retired across lib + MCP tool + CLI subcommand + output schema')
rationale: The finding cites specific ACs from both children showing the same list_epics lib-shim deletion is owned by two children (7c92's shim list and 5899's full-family retirement), a real scope-overlap/coherence issue with an actionable dedup fix.

finding: The plan specifies a 'RebarBotNava contents:write PAT' for the git push to GitHub but does not declare whether this is a fine-grained, repository-scoped PAT (minimum: write to refs/heads/tickets on navapbc/rebar only) or a classic OAuth PAT (account-wide write access to all repos). A classic PAT would grant far broader write access than the single-branch push requires, violating least-privilege on the new boundary-crossing credential path. The access posture is undeclared.
suggested_fix: In `infra/terraform/ssm.tf`, update the inline comment for `reviewbot-tickets-pat` to read: 'A GitHub **fine-grained** PAT for the RebarBotNava account, scoped to the navapbc/rebar repository with Contents: Read and write permission ONLY (not a classic PAT). Do not use a classic PAT — scope must be repo-specific to satisfy least-privilege.' Mirror this note in the Section D deploy-prerequisite description.
location: Section D (Infra) — SSM slot 'reviewbot-tickets-pat'; Implementation precision section — 'git credential helper'; AC bullet 4 ('reviewbot-tickets-pat' SSM slot)
rationale: The plan section given never actually mentions an SSM slot 'reviewbot-tickets-pat', a git credential helper, or an AC bullet describing a PAT — the cited locations don't appear in the plan text, so the finding asserts a PAT-scope defect that isn't present in the reviewed plan.

finding: The plan's caller enumeration names `reconcile_once` as the primary caller to verify, but the actual direct callers of invariant functions are in `run_differs.py` (`_run_differs_invariants` at line 170, `_run_differs_report_schema_drift` at line 232). Additionally, `check_dual_identity_complete` itself internally calls `report_schema_drift` at line 279, creating an internal side-effect chain not mentioned in the plan's cascade analysis. If the refactor splits detection from remediation but leaves `check_dual_identity_complete`'s internal call to `report_schema_drift` unaddressed, the internal side-effect is silently preserved.
suggested_fix: Add `run_differs.py:_run_differs_invariants` and `run_differs.py:_run_differs_report_schema_drift` to the explicit caller list in the plan. Also call out that `check_dual_identity_complete` must not internally call `report_schema_drift` after the split — the cap-hit path at line 279 must be converted to return a `Violation` record instead.
location: Remediation section / AC line 'All existing callers verified: violations still result in the same bugs/alerts'
rationale: The plan's cascade analysis only names reconcile_once, but the finding cites specific real callers (run_differs.py) and an internal side-effect chain (check_dual_identity_complete calling report_schema_drift at line 279) that would silently preserve remediation coupling if unaddressed — a concrete, actionable gap in the caller-verification AC.

finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

finding: The authoring model of the guide (auto-generated from the registry vs. manually maintained with parity enforcement) is unspecified, leaving the update-ownership lifecycle ambiguous: it is unclear who triggers a guide update when a criterion is added or renamed, and whether the guide can drift between CI runs.
location: What section: R-5 bullet ('Registry-derived authoring guide'); AC: 'a generator/parity check fails when a criterion lacks a guide section'
rationale: The plan explicitly directs to 'Decide auto-generated vs hand-maintained-with-parity (recommend generated) and record it' and mandates a parity check that fails on missing/renamed sections, so the authoring model and drift-prevention are addressed rather than left ambiguous.

finding: The echo-suppression signal asserts `mutation_count==0` on the next pass over just-written data, but `mutation_count` is a field that appears in at least three structurally distinct locations in the codebase (`pass_io.py` pass record, `applier.py` return dict at line 682, `reconcile.py` result dict at line 1166) and can mean different things (local field mutations vs. all mutations including Jira calls). The plan never specifies which `mutation_count` field the harness should read, nor where to read it from, creating an ambiguous state-increment / assertion target.
location: Signal definitions / Echo suppression signal; pass_io.py:47-63; applier.py:610,682; reconcile.py:1166
rationale: The plan asserts `mutation_count==0` as an echo-suppression signal but never specifies which of the multiple structurally distinct `mutation_count` fields (pass_io record vs applier return dict vs reconcile result) should be read, which is a genuine ambiguity in the assertion target under criterion T8.

finding: AC5 references 'the nit-framing experiment' as a committed eval case (fixture + scorer assertion), but this experiment has no presence anywhere in the codebase and is not described in the plan body. The plan provides no specification of what the nit-framing experiment is, what its fixture looks like, or what scorer assertion it requires — making this an unspecified CREATION scope item.
location: Acceptance Criteria AC5
rationale: AC5 mandates creating a committed 'nit-framing experiment' eval case (fixture + scorer assertion) that appears nowhere in the plan body or scope, making it an unspecified creation item with no definition of what it tests or its expected behavior.

finding: Once envelope-bearing attestations are written to the ticket store, a full code revert (back to the pre-expand version) would leave those records unverifiable by the legacy HMAC verify path — the plan has no explicit back-out plan for this data-mutation scenario.
suggested_fix: Add a 'Rollback' subsection to Scope: 'If this story is reverted after op-cert attestations have been written, those attestations will read as unsigned on the reverted HMAC-only verify path. Recovery: re-run the affected gate on each impacted ticket; HMAC attestations are re-earned immediately. No data migration is required because the attestations map is last-writer-wins and the old kind key is reused.'
location: What section (global seam repoint, read-both), Scope, Acceptance Criteria generally
rationale: The plan is an expand-phase data mutation (writing envelope-bearing op-cert attestations) with no back-out plan for the scenario where the code is reverted while op-cert records remain in the store; T4 rollback/back-out consideration genuinely applies and is absent.

finding: The unit test AC for the shared-secret check specifies 'without the secret' but does not explicitly require a test for a *wrong* (present but invalid) secret value. For a constant-time-compare gate, these are distinct cases: an absent header vs. a header with a wrong value. Failing to explicitly cover the wrong-value case means the test suite could pass even if the constant-time compare is misconfigured (e.g., always-truthy on non-empty string).
suggested_fix: Extend AC2: 'with the secret header present but containing an incorrect value, the identity header is also stripped and the request returns 401 — confirming the constant-time compare rejects wrong values, not just absent ones.'
location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2 (unit test for middleware/verifier)
rationale: AC2 only tests absent-secret and valid-secret cases; the wrong-value case is genuinely distinct for a constant-time compare and its absence is a real testing gap the finding correctly identifies.

finding: The plan's 'What' section asserts it covers 'every actions/setup-python step that installs deps', but the AC explicitly scopes to only gerrit-verify.yaml, _optionality.yml, and test.yml. A grep of the workflows directory shows external-integration.yml has two dep-installing setup-python steps (line 35: installs '.[dev]'; line 141: installs a second job's deps) that are not mentioned in the AC. The plan's all-inclusive premise conflicts with its explicit scope.
suggested_fix: Add `.github/workflows/external-integration.yml` to the AC's list, or add a sentence like 'Manually-triggered workflows (`external-integration.yml`, `eval.yml`, etc.) are out of scope for this change' to make the exclusion explicit and intentional.
location: Plan 'What' section / AC first bullet
rationale: The finding asserts the AC scopes to only three files, but the AC says 'EVERY...across all .github/workflows/*' and the Scope section explicitly lists external-integration.yml; there is no scope conflict.

finding: No fallback or error-handling protocol is specified for the `git fetch origin main` step within the `authorize-source` security gate. If this fetch fails (e.g., network error, token expiry, rate-limiting), the plan does not state whether the gate should fail-closed (block publication) or whether the workflow should surface a specific diagnostic. For a security gate, the fail-closed contract is critical and must be explicit.
suggested_fix: Add to the 'What' bullet: 'The authorize-source shell script must run under `set -euo pipefail`; a non-zero exit from `git fetch origin main` must fail the job immediately (fail-closed), never proceeding to the ancestry check against a potentially stale ref.'
location: What section — 'Resolve the release SHA explicitly and fetch the protected main ref with full history: the authorize-source checkout must use actions/checkout with fetch-depth: 0 followed by git fetch origin main'
rationale: The finding conflates the authorize-source/ancestry step with git fetch; the plan already establishes fail-closed semantics (mismatch fails before build, ancestry via merge-base, preflight FAILS closed). A missing explicit set -euo pipefail note for one fetch command is a minor implementation detail that the plan's overall fail-closed framing and standard GHA step-failure behavior already cover, so it is not a genuine plan defect under T8.

finding: The build hook's error-handling protocol for a partial git failure (git is present but `rev-parse --short HEAD` returns non-zero, e.g. a detached HEAD with no commits, a shallow clone with no history, or a corrupted repo) is unspecified. The plan names only the 'non-git build' (no git binary) case as graceful.
suggested_fix: Update AC #1 to: 'a non-git build OR any git failure (non-zero exit, empty output) writes `COMMIT = None` (graceful omit).' Add to What: 'The hook treats any OSError, non-zero git exit, or empty SHA as equivalent to a non-git environment and writes `COMMIT = None`.'
location: AC line 1: 'a non-git build writes COMMIT = None (graceful)'
rationale: The plan's AC and What only specify graceful handling for the no-git-binary case; a real error-handling gap exists for partial git failures (non-zero exit, empty SHA), making this an actionable T8 acceptance-criteria weakness.

finding: `session_id.py` is referenced by bare filename in the Scope section without its full repo path, while the actual file is `src/rebar/_commands/session_id.py`. Although only one file with that name exists in the repo, the unqualified reference creates a locatability ambiguity for an implementer scanning the plan.
suggested_fix: Replace `session_id.py` with `src/rebar/_commands/session_id.py` in the Scope edit-target list and in AC6's description.
location: Scope section: 'the small vocabulary-alignment edits (`docs/config.md`, `docs/event-schema.md`, `session_id.py`)'
rationale: The finding itself concedes only one file with that name exists, so the bare reference creates no real locatability ambiguity; the What section also gives full context, making this a trivial nit rather than an actionable plan weakness.

finding: The `classifier_legacy_divergence` alerting CODE item specifies a processing protocol ('compare `classify()`'s per-binding decision against the PRODUCTION differ's action for that binding in the SAME pass') but never defines which code paths permit extraction of a per-binding production decision, nor the comparison mapping between `DecisionKind` enum values and 'differ actions'. The plan says 'scope the alert to the paths where it is' without enumerating those paths — the decision rule is not co-located with the alert schema.
location: Remaining CODE — classifier-vs-legacy divergence alerting; AC: 'classifier_legacy_divergence emitted with the typed fields above (or scoped+documented where per-binding legacy decisions aren't extractable)'
rationale: The plan's own code-verified facts establish there is NO production path where classify()'s per-binding decision and the field-differ's action coexist for the same active binding (binding_walk skips active bindings), and the classifier_legacy_divergence CODE item is not even present in the visible Remaining CODE list — the finding attacks a divergence-alerting item whose infeasibility the plan already documents and scopes, so this is not a genuine T8 defect.

finding: The plan changes sign_authorship and verify_authorship from signing raw canonical bytes (the delivered bde5/change-561 contract) to an in-toto Statement/DSSE envelope, but does not acknowledge or enumerate the callers — specifically the tests in the existing Gerrit stack (558/560/561/563/576/579/580/581) — that depend on the old format and will break or require updating.
suggested_fix: Add a 'Test impact' subsection to file_impact listing the affected test module(s) (e.g., `tests/attest/test_authorship.py`) and stating they must be updated to assert the new DSSE/in-toto structure, replacing assertions on raw-bytes signatures.
location: ## Why / ## Scope / file_impact
rationale: The plan does not change sign_authorship/verify_authorship; it explicitly preserves them unchanged and adds the in-toto contract as new functions, and it notes the amended commit has no consumers yet (cutover deferred to TS3), so the claimed caller-breakage is spurious.

finding: Parent AC6 ('Each child story's plan-review advisory findings are recorded with an explicit Accept/Reject disposition and a one-line rationale as a comment on the child ticket, and every Accepted finding is reflected in the child's description before that child's change receives its Gerrit votes') is not delegated to or reflected in any child story's scope or acceptance criteria. No child defines the mechanism for capturing advisory findings or enforces the disposition step.
suggested_fix: Append to each child's Acceptance Criteria: '- [ ] Plan-review advisory findings for this story are recorded with an explicit Accept/Reject disposition and one-line rationale as a comment on this ticket; every Accepted finding is reflected in this description before the Gerrit LLM-Review +1 / Verified +1 votes are cast.'
location: All five children — none covers parent AC6
rationale: Parent AC6 defines a per-child advisory-disposition mechanism, but no child story's scope or AC is shown to carry it, so it is a genuinely undelegated parent requirement — a real coherence/delegation gap under G3.

finding: The error/fallback contract for `epoch_for_position` is unspecified: the plan gives the function's signature and the counting rule, but does not say what the function returns or raises when `event_position` refers to a position that precedes all events, exceeds the log length, or names a retired event file.
location: §4 ('Provide a helper `epoch_for_position(identity_id, event_position, *, repo_root=None) -> int`')
rationale: The plan defines a helper's signature and counting rule but never specifies its return/raise behavior for out-of-range or retired-file positions, a genuine gap in the function's contract under a completeness/testability criterion.

finding: The plan's AC line 2 ('The query digest is generated at review time from the ticket's current full text') does not specify which representation of 'full text' is passed to S1. In the codebase, `ctx.plan_text` = `title + '\n\n' + description` (det_floor.py:121-122) — it excludes children. For store-wide overlap detection, the digest may need to cover a richer context (e.g. including children's summaries). Without specifying the field, the implementer must guess and S1's digest may be too narrow or misaligned with how similar tickets are digested in the corpus.
location: Acceptance Criteria — AC line 2
rationale: The plan's What section explicitly specifies the input to S1 as 'the same whole-ticket text _run_plan_review passed to its own passes — title+description+comments', so the representation is defined and the implementer need not guess; the finding misreads a spec gap that isn't there.

finding: The post-rollback schema state of already-issued Ed25519 SIGNATURE events is unspecified; the rollback sentence in AC6 covers policy rollback but not data rollback, so partial-failure recovery could leave the tickets branch in an unverifiable state.
location: AC6 — 'Rollback: the required-environment policy is opt-in and defaults off; disabling it reverts to local behavior.'
rationale: AC6's rollback sentence only addresses policy-level opt-in/opt-out reversion and is silent on the data/schema state of already-issued Ed25519 SIGNATURE events, so the rollback acceptance criterion genuinely underspecifies partial-failure recovery — a real gap under T11.

finding: The static-token record schema defines 'EXACTLY ONE OF token_env / token_sha256' but specifies only two invalid cases ('plaintext literal' and 'missing both'). The 'both present simultaneously' case — when a record supplies both `token_env` AND `token_sha256` — has no specified behavior anywhere in the plan. An implementer must guess: precedence (which wins?), rejection, or warning.
suggested_fix: Add to the secrets-format paragraph: 'A record supplying both token_env and token_sha256 is rejected at startup as ambiguous.' Add to AC8: 'and rejects a record supplying both token_env and token_sha256.'
location: What section, 'Static-token secrets format'; AC8
rationale: The plan states 'EXACTLY ONE OF' token_env/token_sha256 but only specifies rejection for the plaintext-literal and missing-both cases; the both-present case genuinely lacks specified behavior, leaving implementers to guess — a real, actionable AC gap under T8.

finding: The plan states 'both halves' of FP-discrimination guards without enumerating what those guards are; an implementing agent must invent the FP-guard list from scratch, which is a design decision that directly determines whether AC2 ('does not over-suppress valid patterns') can be verified.
location: What section / AC1
rationale: The plan explicitly lists the 8 recall patterns but leaves the FP-discrimination guards unenumerated, forcing the agent to invent them; this directly undermines verifiability of AC2's 'does not over-suppress valid patterns' claim.

finding: The plan's census expand-contract task says 'migrate consumers to decision_census' but does not enumerate which external consumers currently read the 'binding_walk_census' event key. run_differs.py:535 is the emit site; any monitoring script, log parser, or downstream alert that reads 'binding_walk_census' is an unenumerated consumer that must be migrated before the key is dropped.
location: Remaining CODE — Census key: expand-contract
rationale: The plan's final task says 'migrate any consumer to decision_census; then DROP' without enumerating who reads binding_walk_census, which is a real specificity gap for a safe expand-contract that could break downstream monitoring/alerts.

finding: `run_ensures()` and `ensures.py` (the source of both M=registry_ids and the `run_ensures` callable itself) are referenced as concrete imports in the What section and all four ACs, but confirmed absent from the codebase (`src/rebar/_store/` has no `ensures.py`; no `run_ensures` definition anywhere). The plan never specifies the callable's signature, return type, or import path — an executor of WS3 alone cannot wire the integration without guessing WS1's interface.
suggested_fix: Add a 'WS1 interface contract' subsection to the What section: `from rebar._store.ensures import run_ensures  # () -> None, raises on hard failure; from rebar._store.ensures import REGISTRY_IDS  # frozenset[str]` — so WS3 has a concrete seam to code and test against.
location: What section ('FOLD `run_ensures()` (WS1)...', 'registry ids (from `ensures.py`)'); AC1, AC2, AC3, AC4
rationale: The plan explicitly documents the WS1 interface (run_ensures(tracker) -> list[EnsureOutcome], registry_ids() -> frozenset[str], applied_ids(tracker) -> set[str]) and treats WS1 as a completed prior workstream that already landed rebar._store.ensures; the finding's core claims (absent and signature-unspecified) contradict the plan text.

finding: The plan does not specify the module path (import location) of the S1 `enrich`, S5 `retrieve`, and S6 `judge` callables that `_run_plan_review` will import and invoke synchronously. Without the canonical module path, the implementer cannot write the import statement or call site in `plan_review/__init__.py` without referencing S1/S5/S6 stories directly.
location: What / Steps section: 'synchronously extract the query ticket's digest by calling S1's `enrich`... run `retrieve` (S5), run the pairwise `judge` (S6)'
rationale: The plan repeatedly references S1's `enrich`, S5's `retrieve`, and S6's `judge` as callables to import and invoke synchronously but never specifies their canonical module paths/import locations, leaving the implementer without the concrete call sites needed to write the integration.

finding: The plan specifies `scenario=detector rule message` for the DET fallback descriptor, implying `Detector.message` is an accessible attribute — but the `Detector` dataclass (grounding/detectors/registry.py lines 88-152) has no `.message` property. The human-readable rule message lives at `det.rule.get('message')` (inside the raw rule dict), not as a first-class `Detector` attribute. An implementer following the plan literally will get an AttributeError.
suggested_fix: In the DET _descriptor_from_prompt branch, use `det.rule.get('message') or routing.get('name') or cid` as the `scenario` value; document that SARIF-backend detectors (e.g. the gitleaks sentinel) may lack a rule-level message and need the envelope or routing name as fallback.
location: ## What — DET fallback descriptor shape; 'Schema + migration details' section (remediation, run 4)
rationale: The plan's phrasing 'detector rule message' arguably already refers to the rule dict (det.rule.get('message')) rather than a Detector.message attribute, so the AttributeError premise may be a misread; but the fallback-chain concern for SARIF detectors lacking a rule message is a real gap, making the call defensible either way without confirming the exact plan-to-code mapping.

finding: AC item 8 presents two mutually exclusive bridge protocols ('SYNTHETIC blocking finding that flows into merge_findings→Pass-2→Pass-3 as a normal high-severity finding, OR the verdict-assembly forces verdict=BLOCK') without resolving which one is the implementation. These are architecturally incompatible: the first requires Pass-2 to verify a synthetic finding (adding LLM cost and variability), while the second bypasses Pass-3's decision math entirely. The concrete bridge is stated to be 'specified + tested' but is not yet specified.
location: AC item 8: '…flows into merge_findings→Pass-2→Pass-3 as a normal high-severity finding, OR the verdict-assembly forces verdict=BLOCK'
rationale: The plan explicitly resolves the fork in the 'ONE committed design' section, rejecting the synthetic-finding path in favor of verdict-assembly forced-BLOCK; the finding's claim that both options remain unresolved misreads the plan.

finding: The 'out-of-enum overlay_id values are DROPPED (not errored)' contract conflicts with the existing `validate_structured` / `finalize_outcome(mode='structured')` infrastructure: jsonschema enum validation on the full payload would REJECT (raise FindingsError) rather than silently drop individual non-conforming items, yet no filtering layer is specified to bridge this gap.
suggested_fix: Add to the Output-wiring section: the `code_review_base_output` Pydantic contract (registered in contracts.py) uses a `@field_validator('recommend_overlays', mode='before')` that filters out any item whose `overlay_id` is not in `OVERLAY_IDS` before Pydantic/jsonschema validation runs. The JSON Schema for the field therefore uses a plain string type (not an enum), and the enum enforcement is entirely in the Pydantic layer. The AC3 drop-test exercises this validator directly.
location: Output-wiring section / AC4: 'out-of-enum ids are dropped (not errored), with a test asserting the drop'
rationale: The plan mandates 'invalid id dropped' via a schema enum generated from OVERLAY_IDS, but jsonschema enum validation rejects the payload rather than dropping individual items, and no filtering layer is specified to bridge this — a real, actionable wiring gap the plan needs to resolve.

finding: `__main__.py`'s run_pass exit-code gate at lines 376-378 reads only `mutation_failures` from the result dict. The plan adds a new `recovery_failures` key to reconcile.py's result dict and `sync_pass_end` log, but the Scope section restricts `__main__.py` edits to the `steal_pass_lock` held-lock path only. As a result, `recovery_failures > 0` will be silently swallowed by the exit-code consumer — a pass with pending-binding recovery failures will still exit 0, undermining the 'loud failures' goal of AC4.
location: __main__.py:376-378; AC line 4 ('set a nonzero `recovery_failures` pass-result'); Scope section
rationale: The plan adds a new recovery_failures tally intended as a LOUD failure signal (AC4), but the __main__.py exit-code gate only reads mutation_failures and Scope restricts __main__.py edits to the lock-steal path, so nonzero recovery_failures would still exit 0 — a real, actionable coherence/completeness gap.

finding: The unit test plan for the positive case ('detectors register on the opengrep backend and a real semgrep run matches') is ambiguous about whether it exercises the full engine_b → criteria_routing → coverage-finding pipeline or only invokes the semgrep CLI directly on fixture files. If it is CLI-only, it constitutes proxy-validation: it goes green even when engine_b silently skips the detector as 'unsupported_lang' due to a missing _LANG_EXTENSIONS entry — the very failure mode identified as mandatory to fix.
location: AC line 5: 'Unit tests: (positive) the new detectors register on the opengrep backend and a real semgrep run matches'
rationale: The positive-test wording genuinely leaves open whether it exercises the engine_b applicability path (the mandatory _LANG_EXTENSIONS fix) or just the semgrep CLI on fixtures; if CLI-only it is proxy validation that would go green even when the detector is skipped as unsupported_lang, so flagging the ambiguity is a real, actionable weakness under E5.

finding: Leaf AC3's era-validity rule formula omits the `revoked_at_commit is None` (null → still valid) branch that the parent's 'Trust config & era-validity protocol' section and YAML schema explicitly specify (`revoked_at_commit: "<git-sha-on-main>|null"`). The leaf writes 'a key is valid … iff is-ancestor(added_at_commit, P) and not is-ancestor(revoked_at_commit, P)' — which, read literally, treats null/absent revoked_at_commit as failing the validity check, directly contradicting the parent schema where null means 'still valid'.
suggested_fix: Rewrite the AC3 validity formula to: 'a key is valid at the cert's bound `main` commit `P` iff `is-ancestor(added_at_commit, P)` and (`revoked_at_commit is None` OR NOT `is-ancestor(revoked_at_commit, P)`)' — matching the parent's "Trust config & era-validity protocol" exactly. Add a test case for the null/still-active path alongside the existing rotation-expiry test.
location: Leaf AC3 era-validity formula; parent 'Trust config & era-validity protocol' / YAML schema
rationale: AC3's validity formula literally applies is-ancestor to revoked_at_commit without the `revoked_at_commit is None → still valid` branch that the parent schema specifies (null allowed), a real spec gap that could cause active keys to be rejected; surfacing it is correct.

finding: The plan asserts the two adjectives will be 'guaranteed distinct' but provides no mechanism description. With a simple hex-nibble modulo approach (as used in the legacy generator), any ticket ID where int(hex[0:4],16) % len(adjs) == int(hex[4:8],16) % len(adjs) produces two identical adjectives, silently violating the guarantee stated in AC-1.
suggested_fix: When deriving adj2, if `int(hex_id[4:8], 16) % len(adjs) == adj1_idx`, use `(adj1_idx + 1) % len(adjs)` as adj2. Document this rule in the plan so tests can pin it.
location: Scope / Design — 'The two adjectives are guaranteed distinct; deterministic off the hex id' / AC-1
rationale: The plan states adjectives are 'guaranteed distinct' but specifies no mechanism to enforce this; a deterministic hex-derived approach can produce collisions, so the guarantee is unbacked and needs an explicit distinctness rule to be testable.

finding: The plan's 'git credential helper on origin' mechanism has no existing implementation anywhere in the codebase (confirmed by grep for `credential.helper`, `GIT_ASKPASS`, `reviewbot-tickets-pat`, `git credential`). The plan treats this as a known pattern but it is a net-new component. The plan does not enumerate any file where this helper would live or how it integrates with the container's git config.
location: Plan §What: 'git credential helper on origin; never on-disk plaintext'
rationale: The plan's stated 'git credential helper on origin; never on-disk plaintext' mechanism is a net-new component treated as an existing pattern, yet the plan never names a file for the helper or describes how it integrates with git config—a real gap under G1/G2 grounding.

finding: The acronym 'CIMD' is used in the client-connection-paths documentation requirement without being expanded or defined anywhere in the plan, the existing docs, or the codebase.
location: What section — '(2) automatic OAuth when the org IdP supports DCR or CIMD (Keycloak/Auth0/Okta can)'
rationale: The plan uses the acronym 'CIMD' (alongside DCR) in the connection-paths section without ever expanding or defining it, unlike DCR which is contextually explained; T8 (terminology/definition clarity) genuinely applies here since an undefined acronym in operator docs is a real, actionable weakness.

finding: The plan's stated rationale for naming the new field `trigger_likelihood` (rather than `likelihood`) — 'the base SeverityAttrs already declares likelihood=low|medium|high mapped by _LIKE01, so a distinct name avoids a field-override collision' — is based on a false premise. Reading verify.py shows that SeverityAttrs is a locally-scoped class defined inside the verification_model() factory function, and PlanSeverityAttrs is likewise a locally-scoped class inside plan_review_verification_model() that RE-DECLARES all five base fields rather than subclassing SeverityAttrs. There is no Python inheritance hierarchy from which a field-override collision could occur. The new CodeSeverityAttrs will follow the same re-declaration pattern, so the 'avoid field-override collision' rationale is unsupported by the codebase structure.
suggested_fix: Replace 'the base SeverityAttrs already declares `likelihood`=low|medium|high mapped by _LIKE01, so a distinct name avoids a field-override collision' with 'a distinct name avoids ambiguity in the LLM prompt between the existing ordinal `likelihood` severity attribute (low/medium/high) and this new trigger-frequency enum (common/sometimes/rare); the code_review_verification_model factory will follow the same re-declaration pattern as plan_review_verification_model (see verify.py lines 242-255), not Python subclassing'.
location: What section — TRIGGER-LIKELIHOOD paragraph: 'deliberately NOT named `likelihood` — the base SeverityAttrs already declares `likelihood`=low|medium|high mapped by _LIKE01, so a distinct name avoids a field-override collision'
rationale: The finding provides a specific, verifiable code-fact (SeverityAttrs and PlanSeverityAttrs are locally-scoped, re-declared rather than subclassed) that contradicts the plan's stated 'field-override collision' rationale; this is a real coherence/accuracy defect in the plan's justification, correctly surfaced.

finding: The plan names 'typed session/change keyspaces' as a key ADR topic ('typed session/change keyspaces and why local must not seed a change's first Gerrit review'), but the code-review package has no typed keyspace definition for novelty keying — `change_id` exists as a plain string field in `sidecar.py`, and there is no session keyspace definition in `code_review/`. The plan gives no vocabulary for what makes these keyspaces 'typed' vs the bare `change_id` string.
location: What section ('typed session/change keyspaces and why local must not seed a change's first Gerrit review')
rationale: The plan explicitly defines what 'typed' keyspaces means — the disjoint title-prefix schemes session:<id> vs change:<id> matched by the reader — and cites the backing module (sidecar.py); the finding misreads 'typed' as requiring a literal type definition rather than the naming/keying scheme the plan describes.

finding: The marker shape includes `failing_change_id?` (optional field) but zero ACs specify when this field is set, what validates its value, or which downstream consumer reads it. S9 (the only other named downstream besides S4) is archived in the parent epic. This is a premature/unused field with no current done-definition consumer.
suggested_fix: If the field is only meaningful for CI-fail (to identify which change in the stack triggered the -1), say so explicitly in an AC: 'set to the change_id of the failing change on ci_failed; null on needs_rebase.' If no current consumer requires it, remove it from the marker shape and add it when S4's AC requires it.
location: Outcome record section — marker shape: `{change_id, patchset_SHA, stack_id, change_ids[], failing_change_id?, tested_sha, recorded_at, acknowledged}`
rationale: The marker shape includes an optional `failing_change_id?` field but no AC specifies when it is set, validated, or consumed — a real gap in done-definition coverage that the reviewer correctly flags under A1.

finding: The 'Signal definitions' section defines observable pass signals for C1–C4 and echo suppression, but three matrix scenarios required by the AC ('status round-trip incl. lossy labels', 'tombstone/grace', 'idempotency mutation_count==0' as a standalone scenario) have no corresponding signal definition specifying WHAT is observed and HOW it is measured. A test author implementing the harness has no specified observable criterion for these cells, leaving them unverifiable.
suggested_fix: Add to 'Signal definitions': S1 (status round-trip): after a local status change is pushed, Jira issue status matches the mapped value AND the reverse sync produces the same local status on the next pull; lossy labels: fields dropped in translation are logged in a reconciler warning, not silently discarded. S2 (tombstone/grace): a tombstoned binding survives for the configured grace period (assert binding still present at T+grace−1s) then is purged (assert gone at T+grace+1s). S3 (idempotency): a second reconciler pass over the just-written state emits mutation_count==0 (no field writes, no comment writes).
location: Signal definitions section + AC line: 'One observable pass criterion PER matrix scenario (C1-C4, echo, status round-trip incl. lossy labels, tombstone/grace, idempotency mutation_count==0, blast-radius trip)'
rationale: The plan's Signal definitions section explicitly defines observable criteria for status round-trip incl. lossy labels (blocked->Jira status+label->restore, no oscillation over N=3), tombstone/grace (404 on N consecutive passes -> soft-retire, single 404 no retire), and idempotency (mutation_count==0 on next pass over just-written data), so the asserted missing definitions do not exist.

finding: The leaf's AC1 delivers only the MCP_ENV_VARS/server.json manifest drift gate for the new [mcp.auth] config keys, but omits the 'regenerated docs/mcp-reference.md / docs/env-vars.md' half that parent AC6 mandates for every story adding new config keys and env vars. No sibling story is identified as the owner of docs regeneration for the auth config additions (S7 is scoped to mcp-auth.md + ADR, not the reference docs).
suggested_fix: Append to AC1: '…and `docs/mcp-reference.md` / `docs/env-vars.md` are regenerated to include the new `REBAR_MCP_AUTH_*` env vars (observable oracle: `make docs` or equivalent produces no diff).'
location: Leaf AC1 vs. Parent epic AC6
rationale: The plan's What section explicitly includes regenerating docs/mcp-reference.md and docs/env-vars.md for the new auth env vars, so the finding's assertion that this half is omitted is spurious.

finding: The plan says `digest_freshness` is injected into the dict `_cmd_show` prints, covering both the default and `--output llm` arms, but does not specify whether `digest_freshness` should appear in the `--output llm` output. Since `to_llm(state)` passes unrecognized keys not in `OMIT_KEYS` through verbatim (confirmed in llm_format.py), injecting `digest_freshness` into `state` before the format branch causes it to appear in LLM output too — creating a show-vs-list LLM-output shape divergence the plan claims not to introduce. The plan explicitly invokes 'parity invariant' logic but leaves this edge case unresolved.
suggested_fix: Scope the `digest_freshness` injection to the `else` (default JSON) branch only: after `if fmt == 'llm': print(json.dumps(to_llm(state), ...))` / `else: state['digest_freshness'] = ...; print(json.dumps(state, ...))`. This keeps LLM output shape identical between `show` and `list` and matches the `managed_refs` selective-pop precedent.
location: show freshness render section; `src/rebar/_engine_support/reads_cli.py::_cmd_show` (~line 168-171); `src/rebar/reducer/llm_format.py::to_llm`
rationale: The plan explicitly states digest_freshness is injected for both the default json.dumps(state) arm and the to_llm(state) --output llm arm, so the edge case is resolved, not left unresolved; the finding misreads the plan.

finding: The rebase-selection rule in AC2 specifies that 'a merge commit that is also a chain tip is treated as a chain' but provides no detection protocol for identifying this combined case — the two constituent facts (merge commit detection, chain-tip detection) use different API calls that are not reconciled in a co-located rule.
suggested_fix: Specify: evaluate chain membership (SUBMITTED_TOGETHER set size > 1) first; if true, use `rebase:chain` regardless of merge-commit status. Only fall through to the merge-commit branch if the change is a lone non-chain change.
location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2 (Rebase-selection rule)
rationale: The finding critiques AC2, but this story-level plan's acceptance criteria are AC-based bullets pointing to child tasks (S2a explicitly owns 'rebase vs rebase:chain vs single-change routing'); there is no AC2 'rebase-selection rule' in this plan text, and the plan explicitly defers implementation detail to children, so the criticized co-location gap does not exist here.

finding: The base allowlist (`pip`, `setuptools`, `wheel`) in `scripts/check_build_env_locked.py` is hardcoded in the script body. The plan describes it as 'small documented base allowlist' but provides no configurable mechanism (e.g., a CLI flag, a config section, or an env var) to extend it. If `ubuntu-latest` adds or removes ambient packages in future runner images, a developer must edit the script source rather than a configuration entry.
suggested_fix: Accept an optional `--allowlist` CLI argument (comma-separated package names, case-insensitive); default to `pip,setuptools,wheel`. The `release.yml` step that invokes the script passes `--allowlist pip,setuptools,wheel` explicitly, making the allowlist visible in the workflow file rather than hidden in script internals.
location: Scope — `scripts/check_build_env_locked.py`; What — 'Hatchling enforcement' subsection: 'modulo a small documented base allowlist (`pip`, `setuptools`, `wheel`)'
rationale: T5e concerns plan quality on the criterion, but hardcoding a small documented base allowlist in a guard script is a reasonable design choice; demanding a configurable CLI flag is a gold-plating suggestion, not a real defect in the plan as written.

finding: The `/user/search` bootstrap section specifies that ZERO or TWO-OR-MORE exact email matches are treated as NO result (ambiguous → do not guess), but AC4 only tests a stubbed hit and a stubbed miss — it does not include a test for the multi-match case (two results with the same email). The multi-match detection is custom filtering logic that could silently mis-assign if wrong.
suggested_fix: In AC4, add: 'a stubbed /user/search returning two entries with the same emailAddress (multi-match) also degrades to the search/unassigned path without resolving to either accountId (test three: hit, miss, ambiguous-multi-match).'
location: Section 3 (What) / AC4
rationale: The plan explicitly specifies custom multi-match (≥2 exact) detection as NO-result logic in Section 3, but AC4 (per the finding) only covers hit and miss cases, leaving the ambiguity-handling branch untested — a genuine acceptance-criterion gap on non-trivial custom filtering logic.

finding: The plan names `grep-ast` as a current grounding oracle backend alongside `ast-grep` and `ctags` ('ast-grep / ctags / grep-ast, per docs/grounding.md'). The codebase spike experiment (docs/experiments/code-grounding-spike/README.md) definitively established that grep-ast is NOT a repo-wide indexer and was retired from the design — the actual backends are universal-ctags (T1 refutation, resolve.py) and ast-grep/opengrep (Engine B, engine_b.py). Naming a non-existent backend causes the implementer to attempt to use or depend on a tool the architecture has already rejected.
suggested_fix: Change 'ast-grep / ctags / grep-ast, per docs/grounding.md' to 'universal-ctags (T1 index, resolve.py) / ast-grep (Engine B structural, engine_b.py), per docs/grounding.md — grep-ast was retired (spike RESULT 1)'.
location: Detector mechanism section: 'ast-grep / ctags / grep-ast, per docs/grounding.md'
rationale: The quoted text and 'Detector mechanism section' do not exist in this plan, which explicitly ships no detector; the finding is spurious/misattributed, so surfacing it was noise.

finding: The plan provisions an EBS volume (Gerrit's persistent data store) and an EC2 instance but makes no mention of `prevent_destroy` lifecycle guards on these stateful resources, nor of any dev/staging environment to test the Terraform before applying to a client's production account.
suggested_fix: Add a callout box in Step 2: 'The EBS volume holding Gerrit data MUST have `lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true }`. Verify this in `infra/main.tf` before running apply. We also recommend a dry-run in a sandbox AWS account first.'
location: What section, Step 2 — 'EC2 + EBS'; Scope section; AC line 1
rationale: The ticket is explicitly documentation-only (Scope: 'Out: any NEW infrastructure or code; S8 reuses and references the d251 artifacts'). The plan does not create or modify EBS/EC2 resources, so demanding `prevent_destroy` guards and sandbox testing imports concerns outside the plan's scope; the finding misreads a doc-writing task as an infra-provisioning task.

finding: The bespoke pass-4 coach path in passes.py (widened from advisory-only to blocking+advisory, with decision map added to render_coach_notes) is a described implementation step but no AC oracle explicitly exercises this code path in isolation. AC1's oracle (`pytest -k 'coach'`) may or may not cover it depending on which test file is written, and AC2 explicitly covers the workflow op path. If a future test only exercises the workflow op path (plan_review_coach), the passes.py bespoke path could regress silently.
suggested_fix: Add an AC such as: 'Unit test directly invoking the bespoke pass-4 coach in passes.py (not via workflow ops) with a verdict containing 1 blocking + 1 advisory finding asserts 2 coaching entries where the blocking entry carries decision="block" and a non-None guide_url; `python -m pytest tests/unit/passes/ -k "pass4_coach" -x` green.'
location: What section / passes.py bespoke path + AC1 oracle
rationale: The plan explicitly widens pass4_coach in passes.py (default blocking=(), forwarding to coach()) and notes it is exercised only by unit tests, yet the plan's Accept section provides no explicit AC oracle directly targeting that bespoke path—so a real coverage gap under E6 (oracle strictness) is correctly surfaced.

finding: docs/plan-review-gate.md is listed in Scope as an in-scope deliverable ('the cross-gate posture note') but no Acceptance Criterion verifies that it was updated, leaving it an orphan scope item with no testable done-condition.
suggested_fix: Add to AC3 or as AC3b: 'docs/plan-review-gate.md is updated to reflect that the `security` criterion is now blocking (not advisory-only); the cross-gate posture note names the new approved blocking set {secret-detection, high-critical-security, security}.'
location: Scope section: 'docs/plan-review-gate.md (the cross-gate posture note)'; AC list (AC1–AC7)
rationale: Scope names docs/plan-review-gate.md as an in-scope deliverable (the cross-gate posture note), yet none of AC1–AC7 verifies it was updated, leaving it an untestable orphan scope item—a genuine E1 gap.

finding: The ADR AC requires documenting 'the three rejected alternatives' but the plan never names or describes them, leaving the executing agent to reconstruct design history independently or invent plausible alternatives.
suggested_fix: Add a sub-list under the ADR bullet: e.g., 'Rejected: (1) always run a full re-review on any remediation; (2) embed novelty detection inline in Pass-1 rather than a separate sub-call; (3) use a probabilistic drop threshold instead of a deterministic floor.'
location: AC: 'An ADR under `docs/adr/` records the design, 3 rejected alternatives, and the invariants'
rationale: The AC requires documenting three specific rejected alternatives but the plan never names or describes any of them, leaving the executing agent to invent them—a genuine vague/underspecified acceptance criterion under E2.

finding: The invocation mechanism (polling vs webhook, polling interval) is unspecified, leaving the executor to make a load-bearing design choice with no stated default.
suggested_fix: Add to the 'What' section: 'Polling loop with a configurable interval (default: 30 s); reads `gerrit stream-events` or falls back to REST polling — decision: REST polling for Phase 1 simplicity.'
location: What / Scope sections
rationale: The plan explicitly fixes a 15 s poll interval and specifies polling against the Gerrit REST API, so the invocation mechanism and default are stated — the finding misreads the plan.

finding: No concrete validation mechanism is specified for the gate-change working correctly end-to-end — there is no before/after workflow comparison, operational metric target, or integration test that would confirm the bot's Verified-casting and atomic submission behave as intended in a real or staging environment.
location: Acceptance Criteria — all four ACs
rationale: The plan does specify concrete validation — a test asserting the SHA-binding logic rejects members not in the passed run, run_id matching, and documented security invariants — so the F4 concern about missing validation mechanisms is largely addressed and the finding is spurious.

finding: The plan targets `prompts.py:627` `langfuse_cfg` param removal, but `langfuse_cfg` also appears in `resolve_prompt` at `prompts.py:385` (a live public signature), `docs/reuse-surface.md:256` (documented API), and `test_reuse_surface_doc.py:44` (CI-enforced signature assertion). These are unenumerated consumers outside `src/rebar/llm/prompting/`; removing the param from the `:627` helper while keeping it in `resolve_prompt`'s signature is the likely intent, but this is not stated.
location: Finding / `src/rebar/llm/prompting/prompts.py:627`
rationale: The plan cites only prompts.py:627 for langfuse_cfg removal but the reviewer identifies additional live consumers (resolve_prompt signature, documented API, CI-enforced test assertion) that the plan does not enumerate, a real gap in scope/coherence that could break the CI signature test.

finding: `src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/code-review-llm-prompts.eval.yaml` is an unenumerated consumer of `code-review-tests.md`: it contains five synthetic diffs whose `--- a/` / `+++ b/` headers name `code-review-tests.md` directly (lines 34–35, 77–78, 106–107, 119–120, 132–133). The plan does not enumerate this file, so an implementer won't check whether the additive sections land in a position that invalidates any of those diff hunks.
suggested_fix: Add a note in the plan: 'Verify `code-review-llm-prompts.eval.yaml` diff hunks targeting `code-review-tests.md` are unaffected (new sections appended after existing content, not inserted mid-file).'
location: src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/code-review-llm-prompts.eval.yaml (lines 34–35, 77–78, 106–107, 119–120, 132–133)
rationale: Synthetic diff hunks embedded in an eval spec are self-contained test inputs, not patches applied against the live code-review-tests.md; appending sections to the overlay does not invalidate them, so this is a spurious concern under G1G2.

finding: The plan refers to '.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml' format and the 'epic's era protocol' without pointing to where that protocol is documented, leaving future operators no reference for the required fields (public key, env_id, added_at_commit) or the process for adding a new trusted environment.
location: What section — 'pin the environment's public key + env_id into .rebar/trusted_environments.yaml per the epic's era protocol'; Acceptance Criteria bullet 4
rationale: The plan references '.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml' and 'the epic's era protocol' without documenting the required fields or process, leaving operators no reference — a genuine vagueness/underspecification weakness under T7.

finding: The census expand-contract (Remaining CODE item 4) says to 'Emit `decision_census` ... from the same `census(...)` record currently shipped under `binding_walk_census`' — but `walk.census` (the dict logged at `run_differs.py:696`) is a pre-computed attribute on the `BindingWalkResult`; it is NOT produced by calling `classify.census()` at the emit site. The plan does not specify whether the implementation should (a) simply rename the `sync_logger.log` key from `binding_walk_census` to `decision_census` reusing `walk.census`, (b) call `classify.census()` on `walk`'s decisions to produce a freshly-computed dict, or (c) something else. The processing rule — which code path produces the dict that gets logged under `decision_census` — is unspecified, leaving the implementor to guess.
location: ## Remaining CODE item 4 / AC: 'Census emits `decision_census`; consumers migrated; `binding_walk_census` dropped'
rationale: The plan's own Key Facts state decision_census 'appears only in a docstring with no .log() call' and cites run_differs.py:652, yet the task says to emit it 'from the same census(...) record currently shipped under binding_walk_census' without specifying whether to rename the existing key, re-call classify.census(), or something else — a genuinely unspecified processing rule under T8.

finding: The plan states 'No SHA-pinning (no other workflow here uses it)' as a factual premise to justify major-version-tag-only pinning in the new workflow — but `gerrit-verify.yaml` already SHA-pins three third-party actions (`lfreleng-actions/checkout-gerrit-change-action@f87b90a3370e62dad310797fedc7fd3700c75832`, `im-open/workflow-conclusion@8eac7f17381a6917bc04fec3e2c92e70ebc37526`, `lfreleng-actions/gerrit-review-action@a1c036a59b94c8ab89de26d378f6a28cc7378b13`). The premise is inaccurate as stated.
suggested_fix: Replace 'No SHA-pinning (no other workflow here uses it)' with: 'No SHA-pinning for `github/codeql-action` — first-party GitHub action, tag-pinned like `actions/checkout` and `actions/setup-python` in other workflows. Third-party actions (e.g. `lfreleng-actions/*` in gerrit-verify.yaml) use SHA pins — that pattern applies only when consuming untrusted publishers.'
location: ## What / files — pin convention rationale ('No SHA-pinning (no other workflow here uses it)')
rationale: The plan justifies tag-only pinning with the premise 'no other workflow here uses SHA-pinning,' but the finding cites specific SHA-pinned third-party actions in gerrit-verify.yaml, making the stated rationale inaccurate — a legitimate grounding concern even though the actual pinning decision may still be sound.

finding: The parent epic's Acceptance Criteria impose a mandatory process gate on every child story: 'Each child story's plan-review advisory findings are recorded with an explicit Accept/Reject disposition and a one-line rationale (as a comment on the child ticket), and every Accepted finding is reflected in the child's description before that child's change receives its Gerrit LLM-Review +1 / Verified +1 votes.' The leaf plan's own ACs make no mention of this parent-level requirement, and the leaf ticket currently carries zero comments — meaning the advisory findings (20 advisory findings per the attestation manifest) have no recorded dispositions. Under the parent's AC, Gerrit votes cannot be cast until dispositions are present; the leaf plan omits this gate entirely, creating a gap between the parent contract and what the leaf treats as done.
suggested_fix: Add a final AC to the leaf: '[ ] Before the Gerrit change receives LLM-Review +1 or Verified +1 votes, all plan-review advisory findings (currently 20) are recorded on this ticket as a comment with explicit Accept/Reject disposition and one-line rationale; every Accepted finding is reflected in this description.' Then triage the 20 advisories now and post the dispositions as a ticket comment.
location: Parent epic AC: 'Each child story's plan-review advisory findings are recorded with an explicit Accept/Reject disposition...' / Leaf ticket: `"comments": []` / Leaf ACs (no mention of disposition gate)
rationale: The parent epic AC genuinely imposes a disposition-recording gate before Gerrit votes, and the leaf plan's ACs make no mention of it; adding an AC to satisfy the parent contract is a real, actionable G7 gap.

finding: The plan asserts `impact_fn` on `pass3_decide`/`pass3_over_findings` 'ALREADY EXISTS — it landed with the plan-review impact redesign child fishable-apivorous-redhead', but `impact_fn` is absent from the entire `src/` tree. The actual `pass3_decide` signature (decide.py:131–136) and `pass3_over_findings` (decide.py:195–214) have no such parameter. This is a hallucinated existing symbol — adding it is a creation/behavioral hunk, not a wire-up.
suggested_fix: Add to Scope: '`src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/decide.py` — add `impact_fn: Callable[[dict], float] | None = None` to both `pass3_decide` and `pass3_over_findings`, defaulting to the existing `impact()` when None so all plan-review callers remain byte-unchanged.' Remove the parenthetical claiming it already exists.
location: What section: 'the `impact_fn` param (that param ALREADY EXISTS — it landed with the plan-review impact redesign child fishable-apivorous-redhead)'
rationale: The plan asserts impact_fn 'ALREADY EXISTS' as an existing parameter on pass3_decide/pass3_over_findings, but the finding provides concrete code evidence (signatures at decide.py:131-136, 195-214) that no such param exists; treating a creation hunk as an existing wire-up is a real G1/G2 accuracy defect worth surfacing.

finding: The plan introduces a new `path <max-lines>` allowlist row format (its core schema change) but never defines the field separator or parsing protocol, leaving three concrete consumers under-specified: `test.yml` (shell `comm -23`), `gerrit-verify.yaml` (identical shell logic), and `test_module_size_contract.py` (`read_module_size_allowlist()` returns bare stripped lines). An executor cannot implement the gate without inventing the format.
suggested_fix: Choose a canonical separator (e.g., single space) and document it as the allowlist format spec. Provide a sed/awk snippet for the shell consumers (e.g., `awk '{print $1}'` to extract path, `awk '{print $2}'` for ceiling) and show the updated `read_module_size_allowlist()` signature returning `dict[str, int]` instead of `set[str]`.
location: Remediation section — 'Convert the allowlist from bare filenames to `path <max-lines>` rows'
rationale: The plan's core change is a new `path <max-lines>` row format consumed by shell (comm/awk) and Python (read_module_size_allowlist), yet never specifies the separator or parsing update—a genuine under-specification that would force the executor to invent the format.

finding: The client command `rebar remote-cert` is specified to 'SigV4-sign the request with ambient AWS credentials' but no library, API, or approach for SigV4 HTTP-request signing is named anywhere in the plan or codebase. The codebase has zero SigV4 HTTP-signing code today; `boto3` is present only for CloudWatch metrics publishing. The established Python approaches — `botocore.auth.SigV4Auth` (internal, undocumented boto3 API) or `requests-aws4auth` (separate third-party dep, not declared in `pyproject.toml`) — are both real, but neither is identified. An implementer must independently discover the approach, risking either an undeclared new dep or reliance on an internal API that has no stability guarantees.
suggested_fix: Use `botocore.auth.SigV4Auth` + `botocore.awsrequest.AWSRequest` from the `botocore` package that ships with `boto3` (already in the `reviewbot` extra). Name this in the Client section and confirm the `opcert` extra will declare `boto3`. This avoids a new dep while staying on a public-enough internal API. A one-liner example: `auth = botocore.auth.SigV4Auth(credentials, 'execute-api', region); req = botocore.awsrequest.AWSRequest(method='POST', url=url, data=body); auth.add_auth(req)`.
location: Client section: 'SigV4-signs the request with ambient AWS credentials'
rationale: The plan specifies SigV4-signing the client request but names no library or approach for it, and the codebase has no existing SigV4 HTTP-signing code, so an implementer would have to independently choose between an undeclared dep or an internal API — a genuine, actionable gap under T1.

finding: The adjudication-corpus schema's `decision` field (`∈ {block, advisory, dropped}`) and `source` field (`∈ {surfaced, dropped}`) both contain the value `dropped` with different but overlapping semantics, and no processing protocol is co-located in the schema specifying how the two fields interact — specifically whether a row with `source=surfaced` may ever carry `decision=dropped`, and what the TP/FP semantics are for such a cross-combination.
location: Step 3 (What) — adjudication-corpus schema definition
rationale: The schema legitimately reuses the token 'dropped' across both the decision field and the source field with distinct semantics, and the plan (as far as shown) does not specify how a cross-combination like source=surfaced/decision=dropped is handled or labeled TP/FP; surfacing this coherence/clarity gap is reasonable and actionable.

finding: The `hashicorp/random` provider is introduced with zero existing call-sites in `infra/terraform/` (Rule-of-Three violated), solely for `random_password` to generate the origin-guard value. Every other one-time secret in the codebase (all 12 params in `rebar_secret_params`) follows the established pattern: generate out-of-band, seed SSM, use `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }`. A Terraform `data "aws_ssm_parameter"` reading an operator-seeded guard value would close the same API-Gateway-header ↔ SSM loop without a new provider dependency.
suggested_fix: Replace `random_password` with: (1) add `/rebar/prod/opcert-origin-guard` to the existing `rebar_secret_params` list in `ssm.tf` (placeholder + `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }`), (2) add a `data "aws_ssm_parameter" "opcert_guard"` read (with `with_decryption = true`), and (3) use `data.aws_ssm_parameter.opcert_guard.value` as the API Gateway static header value — matching the out-of-band-seed pattern used for all 12 other secrets. Remove `hashicorp/random` from `required_providers`.
location: What section — Terraform bullets: 'the `hashicorp/random` provider is ADDED to `infra/terraform/versions.tf` `required_providers`'
rationale: The Rule-of-Three / consistency-with-existing-pattern concern is a reasonable design critique, but it is a stylistic/architectural preference rather than a clear A1 violation; the plan explicitly and coherently justifies why the guard param is Terraform-managed (rotation via -replace) versus the operator-seeded key, so whether this is a genuine actionable weakness is a defensible judgment call either way.

finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.
rationale: The '## Accept' section is empty — there are no acceptance criteria at all, so the P6 finding about AC/definition-of-done quality genuinely applies.

finding: Remediation AC bullet says 'The dynamic Pass-1 fan-out uses an additive `batch.extra_criteria` templated list', but the body's 'Dynamic Pass-1 fan-out mechanism' section explicitly states that a new `batch` field is IMPOSSIBLE because the v3 schema has `additionalProperties:false` and is immutable, and instead adopts the ProductionBatchRunner approach with no schema change. The ADR-contents bullet also references 'the additive `batch.extra_criteria` fan-out', compounding the contradiction. An implementer cannot simultaneously pass both the stated AC and follow the body's design decision.
suggested_fix: Replace the contradicted remediation AC with: '- [ ] The dynamic Pass-1 fan-out appends activated project-criterion descriptors via `ProductionBatchRunner` (calling `route_criteria(ctx)`) without adding any field to the v3 `batch` schema or modifying `gates/plan-review.yaml`; a test asserts a project criterion runs alongside built-ins in Pass-1.' Update the ADR-contents bullet to match.
location: Remediation AC bullet 2 ('The dynamic Pass-1 fan-out uses an additive `batch.extra_criteria` templated list…') vs. body section 'Dynamic Pass-1 fan-out mechanism (clears G6/T1/T3)' and ADR-contents bullet
rationale: The body's fan-out section explicitly states an additive `batch.extra_criteria` field is impossible (immutable v3 schema, additionalProperties:false) and instead adopts ProductionBatchRunner; a remediation AC/ADR bullet asserting the additive `batch.extra_criteria` approach directly contradicts the design, leaving an implementer unable to satisfy both — a real COH defect.

finding: The plan does not address the concurrency safety of the rename itself: if two compaction processes run simultaneously on the same store (e.g., two workers, a retry, or an at-least-once scheduler), both may attempt to rename the same source file to `*.retired`, with no lock, CAS, or idempotency guard.
suggested_fix: Before renaming, check `if os.path.exists(fp + '.retired'): continue` (or use `os.replace` semantics where appropriate), and ensure the compaction lock (if any) is documented.
location: What / src/rebar/_commands/compact.py:140-144
rationale: The plan already addresses this concern with an explicit race guard (`if os.path.exists(fp + RETIRED_SUFFIX): continue`) plus atomic reversal and idempotency handling, so the finding's asserted gap is not present.

finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.
rationale: The acceptance criteria are notably concrete and verifiable — each names an observable artifact and how it's checked (scorer registered, cannot vacuously pass, specs exist, baseline expectations pass), so the generic 'AC quality issues' finding does not identify a real weakness.

finding: The `.retired` file-rename convention referenced as 'per A1' does not exist in the event-store layer. `compact.py` uses `os.remove()` on source events; no code in `_commands/`, `_store/`, or `reducer/` produces or consumes `*.retired` event files. The plan's SNAPSHOT_INCONSISTENT repair relies on an undefined mechanism.
location: Repair per fault class: 'Repair = retire (`.retired`, per A1) each still-present listed source'
rationale: The plan repeatedly and explicitly states .retired/RETIRED_SUFFIX/the retire helper are ADDED by sibling deliverable A1 (a declared depends_on), not claimed to pre-exist; the finding's premise that the plan relies on an undefined mechanism ignores the stated dependency.

finding: The plan asserts 'copy `auth/auth-host/index.js` + `auth/lib/cookie.js` into `infra/terraform/auth/`' as if the source location is known, but the `auth/` directory does not exist anywhere in the rebar repo, and the plan never names the snap repository, branch, or path where these files currently live. With snap described as 'dead' and decommissioned, the source for these files is an unverified implicit dependency.
suggested_fix: Add to the 'Lambda source' paragraph: 'Source: `github.com/navapbc/snap-demo` (or equivalent), path `auth/auth-host/index.js` and `auth/lib/cookie.js`, at commit `<sha>`. Pin the commit so the copy is reproducible.' Include these files in the PR diff so their content is reviewed alongside the HCL.
location: What section — 'Lambda source' paragraph; AC line: 'infra/terraform/auth/auth-host/index.js + auth/lib/cookie.js exist'
rationale: The plan does name the source location (the snap checkout path `~/snap-oakhart-manual/.../infra/shared/auth/` with specific files verified present), contradicting the finding's assertion that no source path is given; the finding misreads the plan.

finding: The plan does not specify the coexistence window duration or the safe-cutover criterion for HMAC removal, nor does it clarify whether the CI merge-gate (child 3) accepts HMAC-signed op-certs during the transition window — leaving the deploy ordering between the CI gate going live and HMAC removal undefined.
suggested_fix: Add a migration-window section: the CI gate (child 3) should use an `HMAC_ACCEPT_UNTIL` date or log-position config — during the window it accepts HMAC certs as valid process records but flags them as pending migration; child 6 sets the cutover by removing that config after confirming (via a script that scans the merged log) that no active ticket still carries an HMAC cert.
location: AC (HMAC removal bullet): 'existing HMAC certificates are handled by an expand-contract migration (documented cutover window / re-issue)'; Intended decomposition children 3 and 6
rationale: The plan's HMAC removal bullet mentions an 'expand-contract migration (documented cutover window / re-issue)' but never specifies the coexistence window duration, safe-cutover criterion, or whether the CI gate accepts HMAC certs during transition — leaving deploy ordering genuinely undefined, a real gap under an acceptance-criteria/testability lens.

finding: AC4 ('Authoring + preview work for both an LLM criterion and a DET invariant') requires DET invariant execution, but the plan body describes only the LLM eval runner path (story #3, agents extra + credentials). The DET invariant path — which is deterministic, requires no LLM call, and likely uses a different runner branch — is entirely absent from the What/Scope sections, leaving AC4 with no covering implementation description.
suggested_fix: In the POST /criterion/preview description, add a branch: 'For DET invariants, invoke the deterministic runner (no LLM call, no credentials required) and return the same {verdict, finding, rationale} shape; for LLM criteria, use the story #3 eval runner with agents extra + credentials.' Update AC4 to reference this branch explicitly.
location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4; What section (POST /criterion/preview description)
rationale: The finding asserts the DET invariant path is entirely absent, but the remediation sections explicitly describe DET dispatch (exec==DET → grounding scan via det_invariants._run_one), fixture materialization, and add AC covering both LLM and DET — so the concern is already fully addressed.

finding: The concurrency test (AC3) mandates 'two real OS processes' but provides no mechanism constraint enforcing subprocess-based implementation. Because `_store/lock.py` uses `fcntl.flock(LOCK_EX|LOCK_NB)`, which is per-PID (threads within the same process share flock state and a re-entrant acquisition always succeeds without blocking), a ThreadPoolExecutor-based implementation — consistent with the pattern used in `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/state/test_alert_store_concurrency.py` and `tests/unit/test_repo_snapshot.py` — would pass trivially without ever exercising real cross-process lock contention. The invariant under test would not be locked.
suggested_fix: Add to AC3: 'The test MUST use real OS subprocesses (e.g., `subprocess.Popen` or a worker script invoked via `subprocess.run`) to drive both sweepers — a `ThreadPoolExecutor` with in-process calls would share the same PID and pass trivially because `fcntl.flock` does not block within one process. Follow the `tests/integration/test_store_concurrency.py` pattern.'
location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3 (Concurrency test)
rationale: The fcntl.flock per-PID reasoning is correct: an in-process ThreadPoolExecutor implementation would pass AC3 trivially without exercising real cross-process contention, so tightening the mechanism constraint to mandate subprocesses is a real, actionable improvement to the acceptance criterion.

finding: The plan incorrectly states that materialize-deploy-key.sh is called from compose-up.sh as the precedent for wiring materialize-opcert-guard.sh into compose-up.sh. Reading compose-up.sh confirms that materialize-deploy-key.sh is NOT called there — only materialize-g2p-config.sh is (lines 177-179). materialize-deploy-key.sh is called from infra/gerrit/setup-replication.sh (line 86). The stated precedent is factually wrong, which may confuse an executor verifying the pattern.
suggested_fix: Replace 'the infra/gerrit/materialize-deploy-key.sh / materialize-g2p-config.sh — SSM → file' precedent language with 'the infra/gerrit/materialize-g2p-config.sh call in compose-up.sh (lines 177-179)' to cite only the actual compose-up.sh precedent.
location: What section: 'WIRED INTO the boot orchestrator infra/scripts/compose-up.sh alongside the existing materialize/.env-regeneration calls' and 'infra/gerrit/materialize-deploy-key.sh / materialize-g2p-config.sh — SSM → file'
rationale: The plan text is truncated before the actual precedent language appears, so I cannot verify whether the plan states materialize-deploy-key.sh is called from compose-up.sh; the location quote suggests it, but the code facts asserted in the finding cannot be confirmed from the plan alone.

finding: The CLI deliverable `rebar identity key add|revoke` (named in §3, §5, and file_impact) is a user-facing flow with no corresponding acceptance criterion and no documented rationale for the absence of an end-to-end check. All four ACs exercise the library/reducer layer only; the CLI wiring itself is never verified.
location: §3 ('Surface this via a new command/library seam … CLI `rebar identity key add/revoke`'), §5 (CLI wiring bullet), file_impact (`_commands/identity.py`)
